news_summary: Today's technology news highlights breakthroughs in light-controlled magnetism, promising advances in commercial fusion power, emerging quantum protein research, and a call for clear guidance from AI experts.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité technologique d'aujourd'hui met en avant des avancées majeures dans le domaine du magnétisme contrôlé par la lumière, des progrès prometteurs dans le domaine de l'énergie de fusion commerciale, les recherches émergentes sur les protéines quantiques et un appel à des recommandations claires de la part des experts en intelligence artificielle.
news_summary_es: Las noticias tecnológicas de hoy destacan avances en el magnetismo controlado por la luz, prometedores avances en la energía de fusión comercial, la emergente investigación sobre proteínas cuánticas y un llamamiento a los expertos en inteligencia artificial para que ofrezcan una orientación clara.

articles.title: A flash of laser light flips a magnet in major light-control breakthrough
articles.summary: Researchers at the University of Basel and the ETH in Zurich have succeeded in changing the polarity of a special ferromagnet using a laser beam. In the future, this method could be used to create adaptable electronic circuits with light.
articles.content:
Researchers at the University of Basel and ETH Zurich have demonstrated a way to reverse the polarity of a specialized ferromagnet using a focused laser beam. The advance points toward a future in which light could be used to design and reconfigure electronic circuits directly on a chip.

Ferromagnets function because vast numbers of tiny magnetic moments inside a material move in unison. Each electron has a property called spin that produces a very small magnetic field. When many of these spins align in the same direction, their combined effect creates a strong, stable magnet, like the one in a compass or on a refrigerator door.

This alignment only occurs when interactions between the spins are strong enough to overcome random thermal motion. Below a specific critical temperature, these coordinated interactions dominate, and the material becomes ferromagnetic.

Typically, reversing a magnet's polarity requires heating it above that critical temperature. At higher temperatures, the orderly alignment breaks down, allowing the spins to rearrange. Once the material cools again, the spins settle into a new collective orientation, and the magnet points in a different direction.

Laser Switching Without Heat

The team led by Prof. Dr. Tomasz Smoleński at the University of Basel and Prof. Dr. Ataç Imamoğlu at ETH Zurich achieved this reorientation using only light, without raising the temperature. Their findings were published in the journal Nature.

"What's exciting about our work is that we combine the three big topics in modern condensed matter physics in a single experiment: strong interactions between the electrons, topology and dynamical control," Imamoğlu says.

To accomplish this, the researchers worked with a carefully engineered material made of two atomically thin layers of the organic semiconductor molybdenum ditelluride. The layers are stacked with a slight twist between them, a detail that gives rise to unusual electronic behavior.

Topological States and Twisted Quantum Materials

In this twisted structure, electrons can organize into what are known as topological states. These states can be understood using a simple analogy. A ball has no hole, while a doughnut has one. No matter how much you reshape a ball, you cannot turn it into a doughnut without cutting or tearing it. In the same way, topological states are fundamentally distinct and cannot be smoothly transformed into one another.

In the experiments overseen by Smoleński and Imamoğlu, the researchers were able to tune the electrons between topological states that behave as insulators and those that conduct electricity like metals. In both cases, interactions between electrons caused their spins to align in parallel, producing a ferromagnetic state.

"Our main result is that we can use a laser pulse to change the collective orientation of the spins," says Olivier Huber, a PhD student at ETH who carried out the measurements with Kilian Kuhlbrodt and Tomasz Smoleński. While earlier work had shown that individual electron spins could be manipulated with light, this study demonstrates switching the polarity of an entire ferromagnet at once. "This switching was permanent and, moreover, the topology influences the switching dynamics," says Smoleński.

Dynamical Control of Magnetic States

The laser does more than simply flip the magnet. It can also define new internal boundaries within the microscopic material, creating regions where the topological ferromagnetic state exists. Because this process can be repeated, the researchers can dynamically control both the magnetic and topological properties of the system.

To confirm that the tiny ferromagnet, which measures only a few micrometers across, had truly reversed its polarity, the team shone a second, weaker laser beam onto it. By analyzing the reflected light, they could determine the orientation of the electron spins.

"In the future, we will be able to use our method to optically write arbitrary and adaptable topological circuits on a chip," says Smoleński. Such circuits could include miniature interferometers capable of detecting extremely small electromagnetic fields, opening new possibilities for precision sensing technologies.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough where researchers have developed a method to reverse the polarity of a ferromagnet using laser light without heating. This advance has broad implications for future adaptable electronic circuits and precision sensing technologies, representing a meaningful technological innovation with potential large-scale benefits.
articles.personality_title: Scientists flip magnet polarity with laser light, opening new paths for electronic circuits
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Normally, changing the direction of a magnet requires heating it up to break its magnetic order. Magnets work because tiny particles inside them, called electron spins, all point the same way. When the temperature is low enough, these spins stay aligned, creating a strong magnetic field.

**What happened** – Researchers at the University of Basel and ETH Zurich used a focused laser beam to flip the polarity of a special magnet without heating it. They worked with a thin material made of two layers of molybdenum ditelluride, twisted slightly to create unusual electronic states. By shining a laser pulse, they changed the direction of all the spins together, reversing the magnet’s polarity permanently.

**Impact** – This discovery is unique because it uses light, not heat, to control magnets. The laser can also create new magnetic regions inside the material, allowing scientists to shape magnetic and electronic properties on demand. This could lead to electronic circuits that can be rewritten with light, making devices more flexible and efficient.

**What's next step** – The team plans to develop ways to write and change topological circuits directly on chips using their laser method. These circuits might include tiny sensors that detect very weak electromagnetic fields, which could improve technologies in medicine, communication, and navigation.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Using laser light to flip a magnet’s polarity without heat offers a new way to build adaptable electronic circuits and sensitive detectors on tiny chips.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques inversent la polarité d’un aimant avec un laser, ouvrant la voie à de nouveaux circuits électroniques
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Habituellement, pour changer la direction d’un aimant, il faut le chauffer afin de rompre l’ordre magnétique. Les aimants fonctionnent parce que de petites particules à l’intérieur, appelées spins d’électrons, pointent toutes dans la même direction. Quand la température est assez basse, ces spins restent alignés, créant un champ magnétique fort.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université de Bâle et de l’ETH Zurich ont utilisé un faisceau laser focalisé pour inverser la polarité d’un aimant spécial sans le chauffer. Ils ont travaillé avec un matériau fin composé de deux couches de ditellurure de molybdène, légèrement tordues pour créer des états électroniques inhabituels. En envoyant une impulsion laser, ils ont changé l’orientation de tous les spins ensemble, inversant la polarité de l’aimant de façon permanente.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est unique car elle utilise la lumière, et non la chaleur, pour contrôler les aimants. Le laser peut aussi créer de nouvelles zones magnétiques dans le matériau, permettant aux scientifiques de façonner les propriétés magnétiques et électroniques à volonté. Cela pourrait mener à des circuits électroniques réécrits avec la lumière, rendant les appareils plus flexibles et efficaces.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe prévoit de développer des méthodes pour écrire et modifier des circuits topologiques directement sur des puces grâce au laser. Ces circuits pourraient inclure de minuscules capteurs capables de détecter des champs électromagnétiques très faibles, améliorant ainsi des technologies en médecine, communication et navigation.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Utiliser un laser pour inverser la polarité d’un aimant sans chauffage offre une nouvelle façon de créer des circuits électroniques adaptables et des détecteurs sensibles sur de petites puces.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos invierten la polaridad de un imán con luz láser, abriendo nuevas vías para circuitos electrónicos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Normalmente, para cambiar la dirección de un imán se necesita calentarlo para romper su orden magnético. Los imanes funcionan porque pequeñas partículas dentro de ellos, llamadas espines de electrones, apuntan todas hacia la misma dirección. Cuando la temperatura es lo suficientemente baja, estos espines permanecen alineados, creando un campo magnético fuerte.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Basilea y ETH Zúrich usaron un rayo láser enfocado para invertir la polaridad de un imán especial sin calentarlo. Trabajaron con un material delgado hecho de dos capas de ditelururo de molibdeno, ligeramente torcidas para crear estados electrónicos inusuales. Al aplicar un pulso láser, cambiaron la dirección de todos los espines juntos, revirtiendo la polaridad del imán de forma permanente.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es único porque usa luz, no calor, para controlar imanes. El láser también puede crear nuevas regiones magnéticas dentro del material, permitiendo a los científicos moldear las propiedades magnéticas y electrónicas a voluntad. Esto podría llevar a circuitos electrónicos que se reescriben con luz, haciendo los dispositivos más flexibles y eficientes.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo planea desarrollar formas de escribir y cambiar circuitos topológicos directamente en chips usando su método con láser. Estos circuitos podrían incluir sensores diminutos que detectan campos electromagnéticos muy débiles, mejorando tecnologías en medicina, comunicación y navegación.

**Resumen en una frase** – Usar luz láser para invertir la polaridad de un imán sin calor ofrece una nueva manera de crear circuitos electrónicos adaptables y detectores sensibles en chips pequeños.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a glowing, twisting double-layered lattice of atoms symbolizing molybdenum ditelluride, with a focused beam of soft laser light striking the lattice and causing a vibrant, radiant flip in the magnetic spin alignment represented by swirling, parallel arrows reversing direction across the microscopic structure, all rendered in natural, muted colors with an abstract, scientific elegance.

articles.title: The hidden technology that could unlock commercial fusion power
articles.summary: Fusion energy may be one of the most promising clean power sources of the future—but only if scientists can precisely measure the extreme, fast-moving plasmas that make it possible. A new U.S. Department of Energy–sponsored report urges major investment in advanced diagnostic tools—the high-tech “sensors” that track plasma temperature, density, and behavior inside fusion systems. Bringing together 70 experts from universities, national labs, and private industry, the workshop identified seven priority areas ranging from burning plasma to full-scale pilot plants.
articles.content:
For fusion energy systems to run safely and consistently, researchers must closely track the behavior of superheated plasma fuel. Key characteristics such as temperature and density directly influence whether fusion reactions can be sustained. Measuring these extreme conditions requires advanced instruments known as diagnostics, which act as the eyes and ears inside a fusion device.

A new report backed by the U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) calls for stronger investment in the nation's fusion diagnostic capabilities. The document argues that improving these tools is essential for giving DOE and Congress the data needed to accelerate the development of commercial fusion power plants.

DOE Workshop on Measurement Innovation

The report stems from the DOE's 2024 Basic Research Needs Workshop on Measurement Innovation, organized through the Office of Science's Fusion Energy Sciences (FES) program. Luis Delgado-Aparicio, head of advanced projects at the DOE's Princeton Plasma Physics Laboratory (PPPL), chaired the effort, with Sean Regan, director of the Experimental Division at the University of Rochester's Laboratory for Laser Energetics, serving as co-chair.

Participants included specialists from universities, private companies, and national laboratories such as PPPL. Their goal was to determine which diagnostic and measurement technologies are most urgently needed to maintain U.S. leadership in fusion energy and plasma science. The workshop also supported the objectives of the DOE's Fusion Science & Technology Roadmap, which "targets actions and milestones out to the mid-2030s, providing the scientific and technological foundation to support a competitive U.S. fusion energy industry."

"Measurement innovations have led and will continue to lead to scientific and engineering breakthroughs in plasma science and technology activities supported by the DOE's FES, especially fusion energy sciences," said Delgado-Aparicio. "This new report provides substantive findings across seven key areas of plasma and fusion science and technology. We believe it will impact both the public and private fusion communities in a meaningful way."

"The findings in this report are a testament to the critical role of diagnostics in driving fusion energy science forward," said Regan. "By investing in innovative measurement technologies, we can accelerate progress toward commercial fusion energy and strengthen America's leadership in plasma science."

Seven Priority Areas in Plasma Physics

Seventy researchers contributed to the report, reviewing seven major plasma physics topics funded by the DOE's FES program:

Low-temperature plasma.

High-energy-density plasma.

Plasma-material interaction.

Burning plasma created through magnetic-confinement fusion (MCF).

Burning plasma created through inertial-confinement fusion (ICF).

Fusion pilot power plants based on MCF.

Fusion power plants based on ICF.

Together, these areas span fundamental plasma science to the design of future fusion power facilities.

Tougher Sensors, Faster Measurements, and AI Tools

The experts identified several ways the federal government could strengthen the nation's ability to measure plasma effectively. One priority is developing diagnostics that can endure the intense radiation levels expected inside future fusion power plants. Another is creating new techniques capable of capturing the extremely fast events that occur during ICF experiments.

The report also highlights the use of artificial intelligence (AI) to streamline the design of advanced measurement systems. In addition, it calls for building a strong workforce pipeline to attract and train the next generation of diagnostic scientists. These capabilities not only support fusion energy but also reinforce a broader plasma technology ecosystem that contributes to U.S. economic competitiveness.

"Both Luis and I thank the members of the working groups and the broader community for their dedication and hard work in putting this report together," Regan said. "Their expertise and collaboration have been instrumental in identifying the critical innovations needed to advance diagnostic technologies."

Major Recommendations to Accelerate Fusion Innovation

The report outlines several key recommendations:

Accelerate Innovation: Speed progress in measurement technologies by validating and verifying modeling codes, AI and machine learning tools, and digital twins.

Speed progress in measurement technologies by validating and verifying modeling codes, AI and machine learning tools, and digital twins. Establish a National Network: Create a coordinated measurement innovation community modeled after LaserNetUS, potentially called CalibrationNetUS.

Create a coordinated measurement innovation community modeled after LaserNetUS, potentially called CalibrationNetUS. Form National Teams: Assemble national groups to efficiently transform new measurement concepts into working diagnostics.

Assemble national groups to efficiently transform new measurement concepts into working diagnostics. Standardize Calibrations: Adopt a more systematic approach to calibrating diagnostic instruments.

Adopt a more systematic approach to calibrating diagnostic instruments. Transfer Knowledge to the Private Sector: Share diagnostic expertise and operational experience from public institutions with private fusion companies.

Share diagnostic expertise and operational experience from public institutions with private fusion companies. Invest in a Workforce Pipeline: Expand workforce development efforts to meet the needs of fusion pilot plants.

Expand workforce development efforts to meet the needs of fusion pilot plants. Plan Now for Remote Operations: Address the diagnostic tools required for remote operation and maintenance of future fusion facilities in upcoming workshops.

About the Report

The complete report, along with an executive summary, is available online.

Delgado-Aparicio and Regan led the project with guidance from Curt Bolton of FES. Working groups developed individual chapters. The Oak Ridge Institute for Science and Education team helped organize the workshop. Editorial and project management support came from PPPL's Communications Department, including B. Rose Huber, Raphael Rosen, and Kelly Lorraine Andrews. Art direction and design were led by Michael Branigan of Sandbox Studio, with illustrations by Ariel Davis.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a significant and concrete advancement in fusion energy research, focusing on the critical development of advanced diagnostic technologies that can accelerate the realization of commercial fusion power. This has broad societal and environmental implications by potentially providing a clean, sustainable energy source. The story is detailed, focused on a single topic, and highlights collaborative efforts and strategic recommendations to maintain U.S. leadership in fusion energy, making it inspiring and hopeful.
articles.personality_title: New report calls for advanced tools to boost fusion energy progress
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Fusion energy has the potential to provide clean and powerful energy by combining atomic nuclei. But to make fusion work safely and reliably, scientists need to measure and understand the superhot plasma inside fusion devices. These measurements are very hard because plasma is extremely hot, dense, and changes quickly.

**What happened** – In 2024, the U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) held a workshop with 70 experts from universities, national labs, and companies. They created a report that urges more investment in advanced diagnostic tools, which are like sensors that track plasma’s temperature, density, and behavior. The report highlights seven important research areas, from different types of plasma to pilot fusion power plants. It also suggests using artificial intelligence and building a strong workforce to improve these measurements.

**Impact** – This report is important because better diagnostics will help scientists understand plasma more clearly and speed up the development of commercial fusion power plants. Fusion power could provide a new source of clean energy. The report also encourages sharing knowledge between government labs and private companies, which can make fusion research faster and more efficient.

**What’s next step** – The DOE and partners plan to create national teams to turn new measurement ideas into working tools. They want to set up a network for sharing knowledge and standardize how diagnostic instruments are calibrated. Training more scientists and preparing tools for remote work in future fusion plants are also priorities.

**One-sentence takeaway** – New investments in advanced measurement tools and teamwork aim to accelerate fusion energy development in the U.S., bringing commercial fusion power closer to reality.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouveau rapport appelle à des outils avancés pour accélérer l’énergie de fusion
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L’énergie de fusion pourrait fournir une énergie propre et puissante en combinant des noyaux atomiques. Mais pour que la fusion fonctionne de manière sûre et fiable, les scientifiques doivent mesurer et comprendre le plasma très chaud à l’intérieur des dispositifs de fusion. Ces mesures sont difficiles car le plasma est extrêmement chaud, dense et change rapidement.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En 2024, le Département de l’Énergie des États-Unis (DOE) a organisé un atelier avec 70 experts venant d’universités, laboratoires nationaux et entreprises. Ils ont créé un rapport qui appelle à plus d’investissements dans des outils de diagnostic avancés, sortes de capteurs qui suivent la température, la densité et le comportement du plasma. Le rapport met en avant sept domaines de recherche importants, des différents types de plasma aux centrales pilotes de fusion. Il suggère aussi d’utiliser l’intelligence artificielle et de former une main-d’œuvre qualifiée pour améliorer ces mesures.

**Impact** – Ce rapport est important car de meilleurs diagnostics aideront les scientifiques à mieux comprendre le plasma et à accélérer le développement des centrales de fusion commerciales. L’énergie de fusion pourrait offrir une nouvelle source d’énergie propre. Le rapport encourage également le partage des connaissances entre laboratoires publics et entreprises privées, ce qui peut rendre la recherche plus rapide et efficace.

**Prochaine étape** – Le DOE et ses partenaires prévoient de créer des équipes nationales pour transformer les nouvelles idées de mesure en outils concrets. Ils veulent mettre en place un réseau pour partager les connaissances et standardiser la calibration des instruments de diagnostic. Former plus de scientifiques et préparer des outils pour le travail à distance dans les futures centrales de fusion sont aussi des priorités.

**Phrase clé** – De nouveaux investissements dans des outils de mesure avancés et la collaboration visent à accélérer le développement de l’énergie de fusion aux États-Unis, rapprochant la fusion commerciale de la réalité.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo informe pide herramientas avanzadas para acelerar la energía de fusión
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La energía de fusión podría ofrecer una fuente limpia y potente de energía al unir núcleos atómicos. Pero para que la fusión funcione de forma segura y confiable, los científicos necesitan medir y entender el plasma muy caliente dentro de los dispositivos de fusión. Estas mediciones son difíciles porque el plasma es extremadamente caliente, denso y cambia rápidamente.

**Qué pasó** – En 2024, el Departamento de Energía de EE.UU. (DOE) organizó un taller con 70 expertos de universidades, laboratorios nacionales y empresas. Crearon un informe que pide más inversión en herramientas avanzadas de diagnóstico, que son como sensores que miden la temperatura, densidad y comportamiento del plasma. El informe destaca siete áreas importantes de investigación, desde diferentes tipos de plasma hasta plantas piloto de energía de fusión. También sugiere usar inteligencia artificial y formar a más científicos para mejorar estas mediciones.

**Impacto** – Este informe es importante porque mejores diagnósticos ayudarán a los científicos a comprender mejor el plasma y acelerar el desarrollo de plantas comerciales de energía de fusión. La energía de fusión podría ofrecer una nueva fuente de energía limpia. El informe también fomenta el intercambio de conocimientos entre laboratorios públicos y empresas privadas, lo que puede hacer la investigación más rápida y eficiente.

**Próximo paso** – El DOE y sus socios planean crear equipos nacionales para convertir nuevas ideas de medición en herramientas funcionales. Quieren establecer una red para compartir conocimientos y estandarizar la calibración de instrumentos de diagnóstico. También es prioridad formar a más científicos y preparar herramientas para operaciones remotas en futuras plantas de fusión.

**Frase clave** – Nuevas inversiones en herramientas avanzadas de medición y colaboración buscan acelerar el desarrollo de la energía de fusión en EE.UU., acercando la energía de fusión comercial a la realidad.
articles.image_prompt: A glowing, intricately detailed plasma globe suspended above a network of delicate, interconnected sensors and diagnostic instruments emitting soft pulses of light, set against a calm, abstract backdrop of swirling warm colors symbolizing energy and innovation in fusion research.

articles.title: Why ‘quantum proteins’ could be the next big thing in biology
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 03 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00662-1Fluorescent proteins with a quantum upgrade could offer unprecedented views inside cells.
articles.content:
Crystal jellyfish have an eerie beauty: thanks to a natural protein, they emit a faint green glow. For decades, researchers have used that green fluorescent protein and similar molecules to light up the field of biology, tracking what’s happening inside cells.

Quantum computers will finally be useful: what’s behind the revolution

Now these ubiquitous tools are getting a glow-up: their quantum properties are being harnessed to make them similar to the fundamental bits of quantum computing. “These fluorescent proteins that everybody uses as a fluorescent label can actually be turned into a qubit,” says Peter Maurer, a quantum engineer at the University of Chicago in Illinois. The idea “sounds very science fiction”, says Maurer. But the physics isn’t new, and the approach has already been shown to work in principle.

Fluorescent-protein labels are currently one of the most important tools in biology laboratories around the world. They can monitor the location and activity of proteins, sense conditions inside a cell, check whether drug candidates are targeting the right spots and carry out a range of other tasks. But adding a quantum twist offers up fresh and exciting possibilities, say researchers.

Quantum sensors can detect magnetic fields and are exquisitely sensitive, so protein versions might be able to pick up the tiny signals made by firing neurons or flows of ions, or spot minuscule quantities of free radicals that hint at cellular stress or serve as early signs of cancer. And researchers can turn these protein-based quantum sensors on and off remotely, making them useful tools for new imaging technologies and therapies.

Protein labels keep surprising researchers with more capabilities, says Jin Zhang, who develops biosensors at the University of California, San Diego (UCSD). “We often struggle with the sensitivity of fluorescent labels,” she says, so she is intrigued by what as-yet-unimagined science the quantum variants might unleash. “I’m still trying to envision the new applications these might bring.”

The effort is part of a larger field of quantum sensing for biological applications, which observers say is hot and progressing fast. Although the development of protein quantum sensors is at an early stage, the researchers doing this work say that there’s not much standing in its way: some of the proteins that could be used in this way are off-the-shelf, and the equipment for manipulating them is standard fare.

“In the past, it might have seemed like, ‘ah, that’s likely never going to work’,” says Ania Jayich, a physicist at the University of California, Santa Barbara, who works on other types of quantum sensor. “That’s not true any more.”

Diamonds forever?

Quantum physics is currently going through a second revolution. During the first, in the early 1900s, physicists started to unravel the bizarre properties of the quantum world, such as superposition, whereby something exists in several states simultaneously, and entanglement, in which quantum states become mysteriously linked. Now, in the second revolution, researchers are intentionally manipulating individual quantum properties to open the door to information-dense, high-precision applications in computing, communications and sensing.

Quantum computing needs qubits — basic units of quantum information — that aren’t disturbed by the world around them. Quantum sensing, by contrast, relies on qubits that are influenced by external factors, in specific ways that can be measured. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), for example, creates an image by manipulating and measuring a quantum property known as spin in the body’s hydrogen nuclei. Superconducting quantum interference devices (SQUIDs) are used to detect magnetic fields in the brain during magnetoencephalography scans in hospitals.

Can quantum computing crack the biggest challenges in health?

One of the most widely used quantum sensors today is the ‘NV diamond centre’ — a defect in a diamond crystal in which one carbon atom has been replaced by a nitrogen (N) and a neighbouring carbon is absent, forming a vacancy (V). The spin states of electrons in this centre can be manipulated using microwaves and lasers, such that magnetic fields, temperature and other environmental factors affect the light that the electrons emit in precise and well-understood ways. These sensors are extremely sensitive, versatile and stable even at room temperature — unlike many qubit systems, which require extreme cold. Today, sheets of NV diamonds or nanoscale crystals are used in the lab and in some commercial products, mainly in the physical sciences — for example, to map the performance of semiconductors.

By comparison, bioscience applications have proved harder to develop, because living systems are “warm and messy”, says Jayich, whose lab focuses on NV diamonds.

But that field is picking up. It is one of a handful of focus areas at the Chicago Quantum Institute at the University of Chicago, for example, and was given a funding boost by the US National Science Foundation in 2023. And it is the sole focus of the UK Quantum Biomedical Sensing Research Hub, launched in December 2024. “We’re at a really exciting time with quantum technologies, where a lot of the lab demonstrations are reaching a point where they’re ready for applications,” says physicist John Morton at University College London, who is co-director of the research hub.

Research teams are investigating, for example, how to use NV diamonds to conduct nanoscale MRI1 or to improve tools used to track magnetic tracers during surgery2. And, by tweaking the exterior of the diamond crystals so that they bind to specific molecules in blood-plasma samples, researchers have developed experimental HIV tests that are 100,000 times as sensitive as standard diagnostics3.

Plenty of researchers are experimenting with putting diamond quantum sensors inside cells. Maurer says about half of his lab is investigating new uses for NV diamonds and will continue to do so.

But NV diamond sensors have limitations: they tend to be clunky, around ten times bigger than a protein, and are hard to place precisely where you want them. Fluorescent proteins, by contrast, are small and can be generated exactly where they are needed inside cells using genetic-engineering techniques, putting them right next to whatever researchers wish to investigate. “The gain you get from that is huge,” says Jayich.

Quantum glow up

Around a decade ago, David Awschalom, director of the Chicago Quantum Institute, and his colleagues started to wonder if they could find molecules that act as qubits. Such qubits, he hoped, could be produced reliably through chemistry instead of being carved out of diamond or semiconductors. In 2020, his team reported in Science4 that it could get a synthesized organometallic molecule to behave like a qubit, and his team soon did the same with other molecules.

That work led Awschalom to team up with Maurer, who had put his physics knowledge to work on biological imaging, in pursuit of biological molecules that might perform the same trick. “It was essentially the same type of idea, but now with a system that was comfortable going into cells,” says Awschalom.

They zeroed in on ‘enhanced yellow fluorescent protein’ (EYFP), an off-the-shelf product that had been enhanced by biologists for a bright yellow glow. From a physics perspective, this molecule has an electron energy structure that is similar to that of existing qubits, says Awschalom.

Tagging with green fluorescent protein makes the nerve cells in this brain tissue glow.Credit: C.J.Guerin PhD, MRC Toxicology Unit/Science Photo Library

Fluorescent proteins glow when their electrons are excited by laser light and then fall back to their relaxed energy state. Biologists typically insert the genetic instructions for the fluorescent-protein label next to the code for a protein of interest. Then, if the target protein is expressed, the label is expressed, too: shine a laser on the sample and it lights up like a Christmas tree. Variants have been developed with different colours. And protein engineers continue to develop versions that are useful sensors: their light can be affected by pH or mechanical forces inside cells, for example, or by the presence of calcium ions, which are crucial to cell signalling, or kinase enzymes involved in phosphorylation, an important on–off switch for protein activity. Fluorescent proteins with no quantum upgrade can’t, however, detect magnetic fields.

A small fraction of the time, the excited electrons in these fluorescent proteins shift into a metastable, non-fluorescent state called a triplet state (so-named for having three possible spin configurations). This causes the light to dim or blink. “People have known that that happens, and they hated it, because it makes your fluorescent beacon less bright,” says Maurer. For his purposes, this was an advantage, not an annoyance, because the triplet state enables the creation of a coherent superposition of spins — and that makes for a potentially useful quantum sensor. NV diamond quantum sensors also rely on a triplet state.

Awschalom says that, after some false starts, it was a relatively simple task to put the EYFP into the desired quantum superposition state using laser light and microwaves. Once the team understood the energy levels of the quantum states involved, he says, “literally the next day, it was working”. As hoped, the fluoresced light was affected by magnetic fields, varying in intensity by about 30%. The team showed that the quantum sensor worked in living bacterial cells at room temperature5.

There are still plenty of hurdles to overcome. One issue is that fluorescent proteins are generally fragile: they degrade over time as you shine light at them. Maurer says that might be fixable. His team is also trying to boost the proteins’ sensitivity. Biologists had previously developed fluorescent proteins that spend as little time as possible in the triplet state; Maurer says they’re now planning on doing the reverse — creating variants and selecting for those that spend more time in the triplet state. They will also work to see whether, like NV diamonds, these proteins can be used to detect changes in other conditions reliably, including pH and temperature.

The ability to detect electromagnetic fields directly is particularly exciting, says Nathan Shaner, a biological engineer at UCSD who develops fluorescent proteins. “Something that’s really difficult to make is a robust, sensitive indicator for the action potential you get when neurons fire,” he says, for example. “It’s a tiny change on a tiny scale.”
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant scientific breakthrough in biology and quantum technology, where fluorescent proteins are engineered to act as quantum sensors (qubits). This innovation has the potential to revolutionize biological imaging and sensing by enabling unprecedented sensitivity to cellular signals such as magnetic fields, neuronal activity, and early disease markers. The impact is broad and meaningful for biomedical research and healthcare, with detailed context on the scientific development and future applications.
articles.personality_title: Scientists create quantum fluorescent proteins to see inside cells in new ways
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Fluorescent proteins, like those from crystal jellyfish, have long helped scientists see inside cells by glowing under special light. These proteins highlight where important molecules are and how cells work. Now, researchers are combining these proteins with quantum physics to create even more sensitive and powerful tools.

**What happened** – A team of scientists led by Peter Maurer and David Awschalom found a way to turn fluorescent proteins into tiny quantum sensors called qubits. These proteins can now detect magnetic fields and other small signals inside living cells by using a special quantum state called the triplet state. The researchers tested this quantum fluorescent protein successfully inside bacteria, showing it works at room temperature.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because quantum sensors can pick up signals that regular fluorescent proteins cannot, such as tiny magnetic fields from neurons firing or early signs of disease. Unlike bulky diamond-based quantum sensors, these proteins are small and can be placed exactly where needed inside cells using genetic methods. This could lead to better understanding of brain activity, cell health, and disease detection with greater detail than ever before.

**What's next step** – Scientists will work on making these quantum proteins stronger and more sensitive by changing how long they stay in the special quantum state. They will also test if these proteins can detect other conditions like pH or temperature changes inside cells. As the technology improves, it may be used in new imaging tools and medical tests to track diseases or brain signals more accurately.

**One-sentence takeaway** – By turning glowing proteins into quantum sensors, researchers have opened a new way to observe tiny signals inside living cells with unprecedented precision.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des protéines fluorescentes quantiques pour mieux voir à l’intérieur des cellules
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les protéines fluorescentes, comme celles des méduses cristallines, aident depuis longtemps les scientifiques à voir à l’intérieur des cellules grâce à leur lumière. Elles montrent où se trouvent les molécules importantes et comment fonctionnent les cellules. Aujourd’hui, les chercheurs combinent ces protéines avec la physique quantique pour créer des outils encore plus sensibles et puissants.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe dirigée par Peter Maurer et David Awschalom a réussi à transformer des protéines fluorescentes en minuscules capteurs quantiques appelés qubits. Ces protéines peuvent désormais détecter des champs magnétiques et d’autres petits signaux à l’intérieur des cellules vivantes grâce à un état quantique spécial appelé état triplet. Les chercheurs ont testé cette protéine quantique fluorescente avec succès dans des bactéries, montrant qu’elle fonctionne à température ambiante.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car les capteurs quantiques peuvent détecter des signaux que les protéines fluorescentes classiques ne voient pas, comme les minuscules champs magnétiques des neurones qui s’activent ou les premiers signes de maladies. Contrairement aux capteurs quantiques à base de diamant, plus gros, ces protéines sont petites et peuvent être placées précisément là où c’est nécessaire dans les cellules grâce à des méthodes génétiques. Cela pourrait permettre de mieux comprendre l’activité cérébrale, la santé cellulaire et la détection des maladies avec un niveau de détail inédit.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont travailler à rendre ces protéines quantiques plus résistantes et sensibles en modifiant la durée de leur état quantique spécial. Ils testeront aussi si ces protéines peuvent détecter d’autres conditions comme le pH ou la température à l’intérieur des cellules. Avec des améliorations, cette technologie pourra être utilisée dans de nouveaux outils d’imagerie et tests médicaux pour mieux suivre les maladies ou les signaux cérébraux.

**En une phrase** – En transformant des protéines fluorescentes en capteurs quantiques, les chercheurs ouvrent une nouvelle voie pour observer avec une précision inédite les minuscules signaux à l’intérieur des cellules vivantes.
articles.personality_title_es: Proteínas fluorescentes cuánticas para ver dentro de las células de nuevas formas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las proteínas fluorescentes, como las de la medusa cristal, han ayudado a los científicos a ver dentro de las células gracias a su brillo bajo una luz especial. Estas proteínas muestran dónde están las moléculas importantes y cómo funcionan las células. Ahora, los investigadores están combinando estas proteínas con la física cuántica para crear herramientas aún más sensibles y potentes.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo liderado por Peter Maurer y David Awschalom encontró la manera de convertir proteínas fluorescentes en pequeños sensores cuánticos llamados qubits. Estas proteínas ahora pueden detectar campos magnéticos y otras señales pequeñas dentro de células vivas usando un estado cuántico especial llamado estado triplete. Los científicos probaron esta proteína fluorescente cuántica con éxito dentro de bacterias, mostrando que funciona a temperatura ambiente.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque los sensores cuánticos pueden captar señales que las proteínas fluorescentes normales no pueden, como los pequeños campos magnéticos de neuronas activas o señales tempranas de enfermedades. A diferencia de los sensores cuánticos basados en diamantes, que son grandes, estas proteínas son pequeñas y se pueden colocar exactamente donde se necesitan dentro de las células usando métodos genéticos. Esto podría ayudar a entender mejor la actividad cerebral, la salud celular y la detección de enfermedades con un nivel de detalle nunca antes visto.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos trabajarán para hacer estas proteínas cuánticas más fuertes y sensibles cambiando cuánto tiempo permanecen en su estado cuántico especial. También probarán si estas proteínas pueden detectar otras condiciones como cambios de pH o temperatura dentro de las células. Con mejoras, esta tecnología podría usarse en nuevas herramientas de imagen y pruebas médicas para seguir enfermedades o señales cerebrales con mayor precisión.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Al convertir proteínas fluorescentes en sensores cuánticos, los investigadores han abierto una nueva forma de observar señales diminutas dentro de células vivas con una precisión sin precedentes.
articles.image_prompt: A softly glowing crystal jellyfish floats serenely in a dark, abstract cellular environment, its faint green bioluminescent light subtly transforming into delicate quantum wave patterns and shimmering qubit-like energy orbs that intertwine with nearby stylized protein structures, all rendered in warm, gentle hues of green, yellow, and soft blues in a detailed, painterly style.

articles.title: ‘The world is looking to you for clarity’, UN chief tells AI experts
articles.summary: Secretary-General António Guterres told the inaugural meeting of a new independent group of experts on Artificial Intelligence convened by the UN that they have a huge responsibility to help shape how it is used “for the benefit of humanity”.
articles.content:
“Individually, you come from diverse regions and disciplines, bringing outstanding expertise in AI and related fields. Collectively, you represent something the world has never seen before,” The UN chief told scientists on Tuesday at the first meeting of the Independent International Scientific Panel on AI.

The 40 experts aim to help close “the AI knowledge gap” and assess the real impact the frontier technology will have across economies and societies so that countries can act with the same “clarity” on a level playing field.

No-one can go it alone

The experts will provide scientific assessments independent of any government, company or institution – including the United Nations.

“AI is advancing at lightning speed... no country, no company, and no field of research can see the full picture alone,” he added that “the world urgently needs a shared, global understanding of artificial intelligence; grounded not in ideology, but in science.”

Stakes could not be higher

AI will shape peace and security, human rights and sustainable development for decades to come.

Warning of the potential for misunderstanding around AI, the UN chief said: “I have seen how quickly fear can take hold when facts are missing or distorted – how trust breaks down and division deepens.”

At a time when “geopolitical tensions are rising and conflicts are raging,” the need for shared understanding and “safe and responsible AI could not be greater.”

Soundcloud

‘Race against time’

As AI develops rapidly Mr Guterres told scientists the panel is also “in a race against time.”

Addressing concerns that AI is moving too fast, he said that “never in the future will we move as slow as we are moving now. We are indeed in a high level of acceleration.”

Drawing on progress made in another UN initiative, the High-Level Advisory Body on AI, which confronts AI policy-oriented issues, the Secretary-General emphasised that the new scientist panel does not “start from zero”.

“I can think of no more important assignment for our world today.”

Nobel Laureate Maria Ressa is one of the 40 experts on the panel, watch our recent interview with her about the AI age below:
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article reports on the formation of a new independent international scientific panel on AI convened by the UN, aimed at providing unbiased, expert assessments to guide global AI development responsibly. This initiative addresses the urgent need for shared understanding and governance of AI, which has broad societal, economic, and human rights implications worldwide. The story focuses on a significant, concrete action with potential large-scale positive impact on humanity's future.
articles.personality_title: UN forms global expert panel to guide safe and fair use of AI
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Artificial Intelligence (AI) is developing very quickly and affects many parts of our lives, including jobs, security, and human rights. Because AI is so powerful and complex, the United Nations (UN) wants to make sure it is used safely and fairly around the world.

**What happened** – On Tuesday, the UN Secretary-General António Guterres introduced a new group called the Independent International Scientific Panel on AI. This panel includes 40 experts from different countries and fields who will study AI and provide advice that is independent from any government or company. Their job is to help countries understand AI better and make smart decisions about how to use it.

**Impact** – This panel is important because it brings together top scientists to share clear and trustworthy information about AI. Right now, AI is developing so fast that no one person or country can fully understand its effects alone. The panel will help close the “AI knowledge gap” so all countries can work together with the same facts and avoid misunderstandings that can cause fear or conflict.

**What's next step** – The experts will start analyzing AI’s impact on society and economies and will offer scientific advice to governments and the UN. Their work will build on earlier UN efforts and aims to guide safe and responsible AI development worldwide. This could lead to better rules and cooperation around AI in the future.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The UN’s new independent AI panel brings global experts together to provide clear, science-based guidance for the safe and fair use of artificial intelligence.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’ONU crée un groupe d’experts mondial pour guider l’usage sûr et équitable de l’IA
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L’intelligence artificielle (IA) évolue très rapidement et touche de nombreux aspects de notre vie, comme l’emploi, la sécurité et les droits humains. Face à cette puissance et complexité, l’Organisation des Nations Unies (ONU) souhaite garantir une utilisation sûre et juste de l’IA dans le monde.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Mardi, le Secrétaire général de l’ONU, António Guterres, a présenté un nouveau groupe appelé le Panel scientifique international indépendant sur l’IA. Ce panel réunit 40 experts de différents pays et domaines, chargés d’étudier l’IA et de fournir des conseils indépendants des gouvernements ou entreprises. Leur mission est d’aider les pays à mieux comprendre l’IA pour prendre des décisions éclairées.

**Impact** – Ce panel est important car il rassemble des scientifiques de haut niveau pour partager des informations claires et fiables sur l’IA. Aujourd’hui, l’IA évolue si vite que personne ne peut en comprendre tous les effets seul. Le panel contribuera à réduire le « fossé des connaissances sur l’IA » afin que tous les pays disposent des mêmes faits et évitent ainsi peurs ou conflits.

**Prochaine étape** – Les experts commenceront à analyser l’impact de l’IA sur la société et l’économie, puis fourniront des conseils scientifiques aux gouvernements et à l’ONU. Leur travail s’appuiera sur des initiatives précédentes et vise à guider un développement sûr et responsable de l’IA dans le monde. Cela pourrait mener à de meilleures règles et coopérations futures.

**Message clé en une phrase** – Le nouveau panel indépendant de l’ONU réunit des experts mondiaux pour offrir des conseils clairs et basés sur la science en vue d’une utilisation sûre et équitable de l’intelligence artificielle.
articles.personality_title_es: La ONU crea un panel global de expertos para guiar el uso seguro y justo de la IA
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La inteligencia artificial (IA) está avanzando muy rápido y afecta muchos aspectos de nuestras vidas, como el empleo, la seguridad y los derechos humanos. Debido a su poder y complejidad, las Naciones Unidas (ONU) quieren asegurarse de que la IA se use de manera segura y justa en todo el mundo.

**Qué pasó** – El martes, el Secretario General de la ONU, António Guterres, presentó un nuevo grupo llamado Panel Científico Internacional Independiente sobre IA. Este panel incluye a 40 expertos de diferentes países y campos que estudiarán la IA y darán consejos independientes de cualquier gobierno o empresa. Su trabajo es ayudar a los países a entender mejor la IA y tomar decisiones inteligentes.

**Impacto** – Este panel es importante porque reúne a científicos destacados para compartir información clara y confiable sobre la IA. Ahora mismo, la IA avanza tan rápido que ninguna persona o país puede entender todos sus efectos por sí solo. El panel ayudará a cerrar la “brecha de conocimiento sobre IA” para que todos los países trabajen con los mismos hechos y eviten malentendidos que causan miedo o conflictos.

**Próximo paso** – Los expertos comenzarán a analizar el impacto de la IA en la sociedad y la economía y ofrecerán consejos científicos a los gobiernos y a la ONU. Su trabajo se basará en esfuerzos previos y busca guiar un desarrollo seguro y responsable de la IA en todo el mundo. Esto podría llevar a mejores reglas y cooperación futura.

**Conclusión en una frase** – El nuevo panel independiente de la ONU reúne a expertos globales para ofrecer orientación clara y basada en la ciencia para el uso seguro y justo de la inteligencia artificial.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a diverse constellation of glowing, interconnected neurons and circuits forming a unified globe, symbolizing global AI experts collaboratively illuminating the world with clarity, set against a soft, natural palette of earth tones and gentle blues, evoking optimism and shared scientific understanding without depicting any human figures.
